stories.headline: Construction Worker Dies at Denver Broncos Training Facility Site in Centennial
stories.summary:
A construction worker died Monday evening after being found unresponsive by coworkers in a "confined ceiling area" at the Denver Broncos' new training facility under construction at 13409 E. Broncos Parkway in Centennial. Deputies from the Arapahoe County Sheriff's Office and firefighters from South Metro Fire Rescue responded to the scene around 8:20 p.m.

The worker's identity has not been released, and the cause of death has not been determined. An investigation is underway by the sheriff's office.

The facility is the Broncos' new training complex, which the team has been building ahead of future training camp seasons. The death marks a tragic development at a high-profile construction project in the south metro area.

stories.headline: Avalanche Trade Ross Colton to Nashville Predators for Goalie, Two Draft Picks
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche traded forward Ross Colton to the Nashville Predators on Tuesday in exchange for goalie Magnus Chrona and third-round picks in the 2026 and 2027 NHL Drafts. The Predators also received goalie Isak Posch as part of the deal.

Colton, 29, recorded 24 points in 73 regular-season games and five points in 11 playoff games for Colorado this past season. He has one year remaining on the four-year contract he signed with the Avalanche in July 2023 and can become an unrestricted free agent after next season.

The move is the first significant roster transaction made by Nashville's new front office led by GM Chris MacFarland, who spent four seasons as Colorado's general manager before departing. Chrona, 25, played 25 games in the AHL this season for Milwaukee and appeared in nine NHL games for San Jose during the 2023-24 season.

stories.headline: Denver Post Owner Pays City $13.5 Million to Settle Lease Dispute, Name to Come Down from Building
stories.summary:
The City and County of Denver announced Tuesday it reached a settlement with DP Media Network LLC — the legal entity of The Denver Post — over a dispute involving the Post's former headquarters at 101 W. Colfax Ave. Under the agreement, DP Media Network will pay the city $13.5 million to terminate its master lease on the building three and a half years early, covering missed rent payments that began in August 2025.

As part of the deal, the "Denver Post" signage atop the building will be removed, and the city will gain control over the building's parking revenue and several floors currently under lease. The Post's monthly rent had reached nearly $650,000, with late fees of $32,000 accumulating since the payment default.

The city purchased the 11-story, roughly 306,000-square-foot building from private owner Kayan LLC in 2024 for $88.5 million with plans to convert it into a courthouse. The Post has not operated from the downtown building since 2018, when it relocated employees to its Adams County printing facility. Mayor Mike Johnston called the settlement "a great deal for Denver."

stories.headline: Colorado Real Estate Commission Sues Unlicensed Home-Buying Operation Active in 21 Municipalities
stories.summary:
The Colorado Real Estate Commission filed a lawsuit against Good Neighbor Solutions LLC, which operates under the name We Buy Homes 365, alleging the company acted as an unlicensed real estate broker in a home-purchasing scheme operating across 21 municipalities in the state. State regulators are seeking an injunction to halt the company's activities.

The commission contends the company engaged in predatory home investment practices that require a licensed broker under Colorado law. Denver city officials have separately indicated interest in pursuing legislation targeting similar operations.

The case is part of broader regulatory scrutiny of so-called "we buy homes" companies, which target homeowners — often those in financial distress — with quick cash offers below market value. Colorado joins a number of states that have moved to tighten oversight of such businesses.

stories.headline: Boulder Quantum Computing Firm Atom Computing Raises $100 Million in Venture Capital
stories.summary:
Atom Computing, a quantum computing company based in Boulder, announced it has raised $100 million in a new venture capital funding round. The raise signals continued investor confidence in Colorado's growing technology sector, particularly in the emerging field of quantum computing.

Details about the specific investors involved in the round were not immediately disclosed. Atom Computing has been developing neutral-atom-based quantum computers and has positioned itself as a significant player in the national quantum technology landscape.

Colorado has increasingly become a hub for quantum computing research and commercialization, home to federal laboratories, university programs, and a cluster of startups working in the space. The new funding is expected to support further development and scaling of Atom Computing's technology.

stories.headline: Bo Nix Excited About Jaylen Waddle's Explosiveness as Broncos Minicamp Continues
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos quarterback Bo Nix, still ramping up activity as he rehabilitates from offseason ankle surgeries, spoke Tuesday about his enthusiasm for newly acquired wide receiver Jaylen Waddle, saying the receiver adds a level of explosiveness the offense has lacked in recent seasons. "You just can't replace speed," Nix said. "That's what he brings to the table."

Nix said Waddle's ability to turn any play into a long gain changes the calculus for opposing defenses and makes every player on the offense better. Waddle joins a receiver room that includes Courtland Sutton, Troy Franklin, Pat Bryant, and Marvin Mims Jr.

In other roster news, the Broncos waived veteran receiver Michael Woods and cornerback Paul Manning to make room for newly signed UFL standouts Hakeem Butler and Sean Fresch Jr. Left guard Ben Powers has also been absent from practice fields open to reporters, though head coach Sean Payton said Powers is "right on schedule" in his recovery from a torn bicep suffered last October.

stories.headline: Nuggets Hold 26th Pick as NBA Draft Approaches; Mock Drafts Offer Varied Predictions
stories.summary:
With the NBA Draft scheduled for June 23-24, national analysts are projecting which prospects the Denver Nuggets might select with the 26th overall pick. Following a first-round playoff exit — the team's earliest since 2021-22 — the Nuggets are looking to add talent around star center Nikola Jokic.

Projections from major outlets vary widely. USA Today projects Texas Tech guard Christian Anderson, praising his assist-to-usage ratio as a fit for Denver's system. Sports Illustrated suggests St. John's forward Zuby Ejiofor for his switchable defense and passing ability. Yahoo Sports and The Athletic both point to driving guard Ace Okorie as a potential fit given Denver's need for a secondary ball-handler.

The Nuggets lost backup point guard Tyus Jones to free agency after Jones quickly worked his way into Denver's rotation last season. Adding a capable secondary creator has been identified as a priority heading into the draft.

stories.headline: Bow Mar Moves Forward with Plan to Gate Public Roads Despite Objections from Denver, Littleton
stories.summary:
The Town of Bow Mar is pressing ahead with a plan to install gates on public streets in order to reduce cut-through traffic, even after mayors from neighboring Denver and Littleton issued warnings urging the town to pause the project. The small enclave sits between the two larger cities.

Officials from Denver and Littleton have raised concerns about the legality and regional impact of closing off roads that are technically public. The warnings have not stopped Bow Mar leaders from advancing the gating plan.

The dispute highlights tensions between small suburban municipalities and larger neighbors over control of shared infrastructure and public right-of-way. It is unclear what legal mechanisms Denver or Littleton might pursue if Bow Mar proceeds with installation.

stories.headline: Colorado-Born Ultrarunner and Skyrunning Champion Megan Kimmel Dies at 45
stories.summary:
Megan Kimmel, a world-class ultrarunner and skyrunner born in Denver in 1980, died on June 15 at the age of 45, according to the Skyrunning Federation. Most recently a resident of Silverton, Colorado, Kimmel was a prominent figure in the sport of skyrunning, which requires competitors to race at elevations of 6,600 feet or higher on steep mountain routes.

Kimmel won the Skyrunner World Series in 2015, set the course record at the Dolomites SkyRace, and in 2018 broke a 37-year-standing female record at the Pikes Peak Marathon with a time of 4:15:06. Her career included podium finishes in races across Japan, Spain, and the United States.

The cause of Kimmel's death had not been publicly released as of Monday. The Skyrunning Federation remembered her as "an extraordinary athlete and true skyrunner whose legacy will live on."

stories.headline: Red Flag Warnings Issued as Denver Hits Near-Record Heat Wednesday; Fire Danger Peaks
stories.summary:
Denver is forecast to see its hottest day of the year on Wednesday, with temperatures expected to reach the mid to upper 90s under mostly sunny skies. Humidity levels will drop to between 5 and 10 percent, compounding fire risk across the state.

Red flag warnings are in effect for much of the Western Slope and High Country from noon to 9 p.m. Wednesday due to the combination of extreme heat and strong northwesterly winds. Residents are being urged to avoid outdoor burning and any activities that could spark a fire.

A brief cool-down is expected Thursday behind an early morning cold front, bringing temperatures back to the mid-80s, before heat returns Friday and Saturday with highs in the lower 90s. The official start of summer arrives Sunday, which is forecast to be one of the cooler days of the week with a better chance of afternoon thunderstorms.

stories.headline: Denver's Juneteenth Music Festival Expands to Three-Day Celebration in Five Points
stories.summary:
Denver's annual Juneteenth Music Festival in the historic Five Points neighborhood has grown into a three-day celebration of Black culture, community, and history this year, running from Friday, June 19 through Sunday, June 21. The June 19 holiday marks the 1865 arrival of Union troops in Galveston, Texas, informing enslaved Black Americans of their freedom.

The centerpiece event is the 15th Annual Juneteenth Music Festival on Saturday, June 20, a free outdoor street festival at 2700 Welton St. that draws close to 20,000 attendees, featuring live performances, food, and family-friendly activities from noon to 8 p.m. Grammy-nominated R&B artist SiR will headline the main stage. The festival follows the annual Juneteenth Parade, which begins at 11 a.m. at Manual High School.

Additional events include the Southern Soul Party on Friday evening, the Juneteenth Hop bar crawl on Sunday, and a Juneteenth Jubilee at Arapahoe Community College on Thursday. The Black American West Museum at 2001 Colorado Blvd. is offering free admission Friday with extended hours. Aurora is also hosting a separate Juneteenth Celebration with the local NAACP on Saturday.

stories.headline: Bull Elk Charge Hiker on Telluride Trail; Sheriff Warns of Active Elk in Area
stories.summary:
The San Miguel County Sheriff's Office is warning the public about aggressive elk behavior after two bull elk from a larger herd charged a hiker on a Telluride ski area trail Tuesday morning. No contact was made and no one was injured; a deputy responded to escort the man safely off the mountain.

Authorities say encounters with elk tend to increase during the spring birthing season as animals become more protective of their calves. Wildlife officials recommend maintaining significant distance from elk and watching for behavioral changes as indicators of proximity that is too close.

In the event of a charge, officials advise moving behind natural barriers such as rocks, trees, or vehicles to create separation. Pet owners are also cautioned that dogs can provoke elk, and leashes are strongly recommended in areas where wildlife is present.

weather.notable: About 12 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: Someone asked me what I do for a living and I said, 'I'm a comedian.' They said, 'Oh, that's funny.' I said, 'Yeah, that's the idea.'

topStory.headline: Trump Signals Detachment from Ukraine War at G7 Summit as Iran Deal Dominates Discussions
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump told reporters Tuesday at the Group of Seven summit in Évian-les-Bains, France, that the war in Ukraine had "nothing to do" with the United States, describing it as "thousands of miles away" and noting that American involvement was limited to arms sales. His remarks underscored a persistent gap between Washington and its European allies, who have pressed Trump to engage more actively with Russia toward a negotiated settlement. Trump did confirm he spoke with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky on Tuesday morning and pledged to speak with him again.

The summit, which gathered leaders of France, Britain, Germany, Italy, Canada, Japan and the United States, opened on a cordial note before quickly turning to the two dominant crises: the Ukraine war and the recently announced preliminary agreement between the United States and Iran. German Chancellor Friedrich Merz presented Trump with a German soccer jersey bearing the number 47, calling it a belated 80th birthday gift. Trump told reporters he expected to be able to reimpose sanctions on Russian oil "soon," contingent on the anticipated reopening of the Strait of Hormuz following the Iran deal.

The U.S.-Iran memorandum of understanding, which has not been publicly released in full, centers on reopening the Strait of Hormuz, lifting the American naval blockade of the waterway, and providing financial incentives to Iran tied to certain benchmarks. Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Tuesday that long-term negotiations would begin immediately after the agreement is formally signed on Friday at Switzerland's Burgenstock resort. The Wall Street Journal reported that a waiver of sanctions on Iranian oil sales would take effect immediately upon signing, and tracking site TankerTrackers confirmed that at least three Iranian supertankers had already exited the U.S. Navy blockade perimeter carrying a combined 3.8 million barrels of crude oil.

Israel, though not a party to the agreement, remains a central complication. Araghchi stated that the deal requires Israel to withdraw from Lebanon, where Israeli forces continue operations against Hezbollah. Israeli airstrikes struck multiple areas of southern Lebanon on Wednesday, and Israeli National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir said flatly that "Trump's agreement does not bind us." Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu had not been shown the memorandum of understanding as of Tuesday evening, according to a person familiar with the matter.

The broader geopolitical stakes of the Strait of Hormuz's status were underscored by French Foreign Ministry spokesman Pascal Confavreux, who told the Associated Press that G7 discussions included exploring alternative oil supply routes to reduce long-term dependence on the waterway. Trump also invoked the Defense Production Act via a presidential memo, directing the Defense Secretary to address munitions supply chain bottlenecks exposed by the Iran conflict. Republican Sen. Lindsey Graham told CBS News he remained "pretty skeptical" about the planned U.S.-Iran talks, reflecting cautious sentiment among some congressional allies even as diplomatic activity accelerates ahead of Friday's signing.
